title: Economic Bulletin Issue 6, 2025
summary: At its September 11, 2025 meeting, the ECB Governing Council kept interest rates unchanged, with inflation at approximately 2% and broadly unchanged inflation outlook. ECB staff projections show headline inflation averaging 2.1% in 2025, 1.7% in 2026, and 1.9% in 2027, while GDP growth is projected at 1.2%, 1.0%, and 1.3% for those same years respectively. The Governing Council remains data-dependent and meeting-by-meeting, with no pre-commitment to a particular rate path.
key_points: ECB Governing Council kept three key interest rates unchanged at September 11, 2025 meeting; inflation currently around the 2% medium-term target
Headline HICP inflation projected at 2.1% in 2025, 1.7% in 2026, and 1.9% in 2027; HICP ex-energy and food at 2.4%, 1.9%, and 1.8% respectively
GDP growth revised up to 1.2% for 2025 (from 0.9% in June projections), 1.0% for 2026, and 1.3% for 2027
Unemployment rate at 6.2% in July 2025; economy grew 0.7% in cumulative terms over first half of 2025
Compensation per employee growth slowed to 3.9% in Q2 2025, down from 4.0% in Q1 and 4.8% in Q2 2024
Governing Council will follow data-dependent, meeting-by-meeting approach; not pre-committing to any particular rate path
New US-EU trade agreement reduced trade policy uncertainty despite higher tariffs on euro area exports to US
why_it_matters: This ECB Economic Bulletin provides the authoritative basis for understanding the ECB's assessment of the euro area economic outlook. Investors and analysts tracking ECB policy direction need to understand that the Governing Council sees inflation close to target but is not prepared to signal imminent rate cuts, emphasizing a cautious data-dependent stance. The revised growth projections and discussion of trade tariff impacts inform expectations about the euro area's resilience.
macro_context: The ECB sees euro area inflation close to its 2% target with a gradual path to 1.7% in 2026. Growth has been revised up for 2025 to 1.2% but lowered slightly for 2026 to 1.0%, partly reflecting euro appreciation and higher tariffs. The labour market remains strong with unemployment at 6.2%. Wage growth is moderating from 4.8% in Q2 2024 to 3.9% in Q2 2025. The ECB expects domestic demand to benefit from past rate cuts, rising real wages, and new government spending on infrastructure and defence (mainly Germany). Trade tariff uncertainty caused frontloading and volatility in H1 2025 but a new US-EU trade agreement has reduced uncertainty.
notes: Source is a high-quality ECB Economic Bulletin providing comprehensive assessment of euro area economic conditions and monetary policy outlook. Published September 2025 (approximately 6 months before today). This is within the 90-day threshold but approaching historical status. The bulletin presents official ECB staff projections and Governing Council assessment, making it authoritative. Source body was truncated at character limit but sufficient content was provided for comprehensive analysis.
